questions.question: What is the main job of insulin in the body?
options: To help glucose move out of the blood and into the body's cells to be used for energy
To raise blood glucose after every meal
To turn glucose into water for the body to store
To remove carbohydrate from food before it is digested

questions.question: Where in the body is insulin made?
options: The kidneys
The liver
The stomach
The pancreas

questions.question: What is glucose?
options: A hormone that unlocks the body's cells
A waste product the kidneys remove
A type of fat stored in the blood
A sugar that travels in the blood and is the body's main source of energy

questions.question: What happens to blood glucose when diabetes is not treated?
options: It stays in the blood instead of getting into the cells, so levels rise too high
It is turned straight into insulin
It stays exactly the same as in someone without diabetes
It disappears from the blood completely

questions.question: What causes Type 1 diabetes?
options: The pancreas making far too much insulin
Eating too much sugar as a child
A short-term infection that clears up on its own
The body's immune system attacking the cells that make insulin, so little or none is made

questions.question: Which statement best describes Type 2 diabetes?
options: The body still makes some insulin but not enough, or the insulin does not work properly
The body makes no insulin at all from birth
It always starts in early childhood
It can only be treated with insulin injections

questions.question: Which type of diabetes means a person needs insulin every day, for life?
options: Neither type ever needs insulin
Type 1
Diabetes that develops in pregnancy
Type 2

questions.question: Which type of diabetes is much more common?
options: Both are equally common
Type 2
Type 1
Neither is common in adults

questions.question: The phrase 'four is the floor' reminds us that a hypo is generally treated when blood glucose drops below what level?
options: 14 mmol/L
4 mmol/L
1 mmol/L
40 mmol/L

questions.question: Which of these is a common cause of a hypo in someone on insulin or certain tablets?
options: Resting quietly after a normal meal
A meal being missed or delayed, or doing more activity than usual
Drinking plenty of water during the day
Eating a large, sugary pudding at lunchtime

questions.question: If a person with diabetes is having a hypo but is awake and able to swallow safely, what should you give first?
options: Nothing until a nurse arrives
A large plate of pasta
A fast-acting sugar such as glucose tablets, pure fruit juice or a sugary drink
A slice of wholemeal toast

questions.question: After a person has recovered from a hypo with fast-acting sugar, what should you give next?
options: A diet fizzy drink
A longer-acting carbohydrate such as a sandwich, fruit or biscuits
Nothing more to eat that day
More fast-acting sugar every few minutes for an hour

questions.question: A person you support with diabetes has become unconscious. What should you do?
options: Try to give them a sugary drink to bring their glucose up
Wait to see whether they wake up on their own
Call 999, put them in the recovery position, stay with them, and give nothing by mouth
Put glucose gel or a biscuit into their mouth

questions.question: How long should you usually wait after giving fast-acting sugar before checking the person again?
options: A full hour
About 1 to 2 minutes
About 10 to 15 minutes
Until the next mealtime

questions.question: Which set of signs is typical of a hyper (high blood glucose)?
options: A fast heartbeat that settles after a sugary drink
Feeling shaky, sweaty and hungry
Feeling cold, calm and full of energy
Being very thirsty, passing lots of urine and feeling tired

questions.question: Compared with a hypo, how does a hyper usually develop?
options: Within a few seconds
It never develops gradually
Only ever while the person is asleep
More slowly, over hours or days

questions.question: In diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), where do the harmful acids called ketones come from?
options: The body breaking down fat for energy because there is not enough insulin
The pancreas releasing extra insulin
Eating special diabetic foods
Drinking too much water

questions.question: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is far more common in people with which type of diabetes?
options: Diabetes that develops in pregnancy
Type 1
It is equally common in all types
Type 2

questions.question: Which of these could be a warning sign of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)?
options: Being sick, tummy pain, deep or rapid breathing and breath that smells sweet or fruity
Feeling calm and well with a normal appetite
Sleeping soundly through the night with no thirst
Feeling shaky and hungry that settles after a sugary drink

questions.question: Why does keeping blood glucose within a healthy range over the long term matter?
options: It cures Type 1 diabetes
It has no real effect on long-term health
It reduces damage to the blood vessels and nerves and lowers the risk of long-term complications
It makes insulin unnecessary for everyone

questions.question: Which parts of the body can be damaged by poorly controlled diabetes over time?
options: None of the body is affected
Only the ears
Only the hair and nails
The eyes, kidneys, nerves, feet and heart

questions.question: You notice a red, sore area on the foot of a person with diabetes. What should you do?
options: Cut away any hard skin around it to help it heal
Report it promptly and record it, so it can be checked before it gets worse
Ignore it, as small foot problems always heal on their own
Tell the person to keep walking on it to keep the blood flowing

questions.question: Who should cut a person's corns or hard skin on the feet?
options: A podiatrist
The person's family
Any care worker who has the time
Anyone, as it is not important

questions.question: What is the best guidance on diet for most people with diabetes?
options: Cutting out every trace of sugar and carbohydrate completely
A healthy, balanced diet like the Eatwell Guide, with sensible portions and food they enjoy
Skipping meals to keep blood glucose low
Eating only special, expensive 'diabetic' foods

questions.question: What effect does regular physical activity tend to have on blood glucose?
options: It stops the body needing food altogether
It lowers blood glucose
It has no effect on blood glucose
It raises blood glucose sharply

questions.question: Can a care worker administer insulin or diabetes medication after completing this awareness course?
options: Yes, but only at the weekend
No, that requires specific training, assessed competence and a proper delegation from a qualified professional
Yes, as long as the person asks them to
Yes, this course fully authorises it

questions.question: People with Type 1 diabetes need insulin every day because their body makes little or no insulin of its own.
options: True
False

questions.question: Type 2 diabetes is less common than Type 1 diabetes.
options: True
False

questions.question: Insulin raises blood glucose after a meal.
options: True
False

questions.question: A hypo is when blood glucose falls too low and needs treating straight away.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is safe to give a sugary drink to a person who is unconscious to bring their glucose up.
options: True
False

questions.question: Extra physical activity can lower blood glucose, so people on insulin or certain tablets may be more likely to have a hypo when they are more active than usual.
options: True
False

questions.question: A single high blood glucose reading is usually a medical emergency that always needs a 999 call on its own.
options: True
False

questions.question: If a person with diabetes is unwell and vomiting, or has readings that are repeatedly very high or very low, you should report it promptly rather than wait and see.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of these are signs of a hypo? (Select all that apply)
options: Passing lots of urine over several days
Becoming confused or irritable
Sweating and looking pale
Feeling shaky or trembling

questions.question: Which of these are common triggers for a hypo? (Select all that apply)
options: More physical activity than normal
A meal or snack being missed, delayed or smaller than usual
Drinking alcohol
Resting quietly after a normal meal
Drinking plenty of water

questions.question: Which of these are classic signs of a hyper (high blood glucose)? (Select all that apply)
options: Passing lots of urine
Feeling tired or lacking energy
Feeling shaky and hungry
Being very thirsty

questions.question: Which of these are long-term complications that can result from poorly controlled diabetes? (Select all that apply)
options: Nerve damage and foot problems
Damage to the kidneys
Damage to the eyes
Heart disease and stroke

questions.question: Which of these are good foot-care actions when supporting someone with diabetes? (Select all that apply)
options: Report any cuts, redness, swelling or sores straight away
Help them check their feet daily
Keep the feet clean and dry and wear well-fitting shoes
Cut away corns and hard skin yourself

questions.question: Which of these are warning signs of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)? (Select all that apply)
options: Deep or rapid breathing
Feeling and being sick, with tummy pain
Breath that smells sweet or fruity
Feeling calm and well with a normal appetite

questions.question: Which of these are part of your everyday role in supporting someone with diabetes? (Select all that apply)
options: Noticing when things change, such as a hypo, hyper or foot problem
Diagnosing diabetes and deciding on treatment
Reporting changes and worries promptly
Recording readings, meals, symptoms and concerns accurately

questions.question: A ___ is when blood glucose falls too low.
Insulin is a hormone made in the ___.
A hypo is generally treated when blood glucose drops below ___ mmol/L.
In DKA, the body breaks down fat instead of glucose, producing acids called ___ that build up and make the blood dangerously acidic.
Never put food or drink into the mouth of someone who is ___, because it can choke them.
